expanded_description: Learn to determine molecular formulas from empirical formulas and molar mass data. Master the relationship between empirical and molecular formulas and apply this knowledge to identify unknown compounds from analytical data.

core_concept.principle: Molecular Formula Is a Whole-Number Multiple of Empirical Formula
core_concept.explanation: The molecular formula shows the actual number of atoms of each element in a molecule, while the empirical formula shows the simplest whole-number ratio. The molecular formula is always a whole-number multiple of the empirical formula: $\text{Molecular Formula} = n \times \text{Empirical Formula}$, where $n = 1, 2, 3, ...$. Determining $n$ requires the compound's molar mass from experimental data.

image.alt: Diagram showing empirical formula multiplied by n to get molecular formula
image.caption: Figure 8.7.1: The molecular formula is $n$ times the empirical formula, where $n$ is determined from molar mass.

sections.section_title: Empirical vs. Molecular Formulas: The Relationship
sections.introduction: Understanding the distinction between empirical and molecular formulas is essential for identifying compounds from analytical data. While the empirical formula gives the simplest ratio, the molecular formula reveals the actual molecular composition.

key_concept.definition: The empirical formula represents the simplest whole-number ratio of atoms in a compound. The molecular formula represents the actual number of atoms of each element in a molecule. The molecular formula is always a whole-number multiple of the empirical formula.
key_concept.context: Multiple compounds can share the same empirical formula but have different molecular formulas and properties. Molar mass data distinguishes between them.

components.equation: $\text{Molecular Formula} = n \times \text{Empirical Formula}$ where $n = \dfrac{\text{Molar Mass}}{\text{Empirical Formula Mass}}$
components.interpretation: The factor $n$ must be a whole number (1, 2, 3, ...). If calculation gives a non-whole number, check empirical formula or molar mass data.

image.alt: Flowchart: empirical formula + molar mass yields molecular formula
image.caption: Figure 8.7.2: Molar mass determines the multiplier $n$ that converts empirical to molecular formula.

characteristics: Empirical formula cannot be simplified further
Molar mass equals empirical formula mass
classifications.behavior: When the empirical formula mass equals the experimental molar mass, the empirical formula is the molecular formula.

examples.explanation: Empirical: $\text{H}_2\text{O}$; Empirical mass: $18.02~\text{g/mol}$; Molar mass: $18.02~\text{g/mol}$; $n = 1$; Molecular: $\text{H}_2\text{O}$.
Empirical: $\text{NH}_3$; Empirical mass: $17.03~\text{g/mol}$; Molar mass: $17.03~\text{g/mol}$; $n = 1$; Molecular: $\text{NH}_3$.

classifications.value: Actual Composition Is Simplified Ratio Times $n$
characteristics: Empirical formula can be multiplied by integer $n$
Molar mass is multiple of empirical formula mass
classifications.behavior: When molar mass exceeds empirical formula mass, multiply empirical subscripts by $n$ to get molecular formula.

image.caption: Glucose molecular formula is 6 times empirical.
Benzene molecular formula is 6 times empirical.

characteristics: Different compounds can have same empirical formula
Examples: formaldehyde, acetic acid, glucose all $\text{CH}_2\text{O}$
classifications.behavior: Analytical chemistry requires both empirical formula AND molar mass to uniquely identify a compound.

image.alt: Three compounds with same empirical formula
image.caption: Same empirical, different molecular formulas and properties.

principles.principle: The $n$ Factor Must Be a Whole Number
principles.statement: The multiplier $n = \dfrac{\text{Molar Mass}}{\text{Empirical Formula Mass}}$ must equal (or round to) a whole number. Non-whole results indicate calculation or data errors.
principles.application: If $n = 2.98$, round to 3 (experimental error). If $n = 2.5$, recheck empirical formula or molar mass—something is wrong.
principles.analogy: Like counting people: you can have 2 or 3 people, but not 2.5 people. Molecular formulas require whole atoms.

principles.principle: Molar Mass Must Be Experimentally Determined
principles.statement: The molar mass needed to find $n$ comes from experimental methods: mass spectrometry, freezing point depression, vapor density, etc.
principles.application: Combustion analysis gives empirical formula; mass spectrometry gives molar mass; together they yield molecular formula.
principles.example: This two-step analytical approach is standard in organic compound identification.

sections.introduction: Determining molecular formulas from empirical formulas follows a systematic three-step process. Mastering this method enables identification of unknown compounds from combined analytical data.

key_concept.definition: The molecular formula calculation: (1) Calculate empirical formula mass, (2) Divide molar mass by empirical formula mass to get $n$, (3) Multiply empirical subscripts by $n$ to get molecular formula.
key_concept.context: This method works for any compound with known empirical formula and experimental molar mass.

process_flow.description: Sum the atomic masses of all atoms in the empirical formula using values from the periodic table.
Divide the experimental molar mass by the empirical formula mass: $n = \dfrac{\text{Molar Mass}}{\text{Empirical Formula Mass}}$. Round to nearest whole number.
Multiply each subscript in the empirical formula by $n$ to get the molecular formula subscripts.
Calculate the molar mass of the molecular formula; it should match the given experimental molar mass.

worked_examples.title: Simple Molecular Formula Determination
worked_examples.problem: A compound has empirical formula $\text{CH}_2\text{O}$ and molar mass $60.05~\text{g/mol}$. Determine the molecular formula.
worked_examples.final_answer: The molecular formula is $\text{C}_2\text{H}_4\text{O}_2$. This could represent acetic acid ($\text{CH}_3\text{COOH}$) or methyl formate ($\text{HCOOCH}_3$); additional structural data would distinguish these isomers.

worked_examples.title: Molecular Formula from Percent Composition and Molar Mass
worked_examples.problem: A compound is $40.0\%$ C, $6.71\%$ H, and $53.3\%$ O by mass. Its molar mass is $180.16~\text{g/mol}$. Determine the molecular formula.

step_by_step.explanation: Assume 100 g: C: $40.0~\text{g} \div 12.01 = 3.33~\text{mol}$; H: $6.71~\text{g} \div 1.008 = 6.66~\text{mol}$; O: $53.3~\text{g} \div 16.00 = 3.33~\text{mol}$. Ratio: $1:2:1$ → Empirical: $\text{CH}_2\text{O}$.

worked_examples.final_answer: The molecular formula is $\text{C}_6\text{H}_{12}\text{O}_6$. This is glucose (or fructose, galactose—structural isomers with same molecular formula). The six-fold multiplier reflects glucose's larger size compared to the simplest $\text{CH}_2\text{O}$ ratio.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Combined Percent Composition and Molar Mass Analysis

worked_examples.title: Handling Non-Exact $n$ Values
worked_examples.problem: A compound has empirical formula $\text{C}_3\text{H}_4\text{O}_3$ and molar mass $176.12~\text{g/mol}$. Determine the molecular formula.
worked_examples.final_answer: The molecular formula is $\text{C}_6\text{H}_8\text{O}_6$. This is ascorbic acid (vitamin C). The exact $n = 2$ confirms the empirical formula and molar mass data are consistent.

worked_examples.title: Rounding $n$ to Nearest Whole Number
worked_examples.problem: A compound has empirical formula $\text{C}_2\text{H}_3\text{Cl}$ and molar mass $125.0~\text{g/mol}$. Determine the molecular formula.
worked_examples.final_answer: The molecular formula is $\text{C}_4\text{H}_6\text{Cl}_2$. The calculated $n = 2.00$ (within experimental precision) confirms the molecular formula. This compound could be 1,4-dichloro-2-butene or other isomers.

worked_examples.title: Identifying Error When $n$ Is Not Whole
worked_examples.problem: A compound has empirical formula $\text{CH}$ and molar mass $50.0~\text{g/mol}$. Calculate $n$ and identify any issues.

step_by_step.explanation: $n = \dfrac{50.0}{13.02} = 3.84$. Not close to a whole number.
Possible issues: (1) Empirical formula is wrong, (2) Molar mass measurement has large error, (3) Compound is a mixture.
If $n = 4$, expected molar mass = $4 \times 13.02 = 52.08~\text{g/mol}$. Given $50.0~\text{g/mol}$ is $4\%$ low—possible experimental error.

worked_examples.final_answer: The calculated $n = 3.84$ is not close to a whole number, indicating a problem. If experimental error is $\pm 4\%$, $n = 4$ (molecular formula $\text{C}_4\text{H}_4$) is possible. Otherwise, recheck the empirical formula determination or molar mass measurement. This example shows why $n$ must be whole-numbered.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Error Detection in Molecular Formula Calculation

sections.introduction: The multiplier $n$ requires experimental molar mass data. Several analytical methods provide this critical information for molecular formula determination.

key_concept.definition: Molar mass can be determined experimentally by: mass spectrometry (most accurate), freezing point depression, boiling point elevation, vapor density, or osmotic pressure measurements.
key_concept.context: Each method has advantages and limitations; mass spectrometry is preferred for organic compounds due to precision and small sample requirements.

categories.description: Ionizes molecules and measures mass-to-charge ratio ($m/z$) to determine molecular mass with high precision.
categories.detailed_mechanism: Molecules are ionized (often by electron impact), accelerated through electric/magnetic fields, and detected. The molecular ion peak ($M^+$) gives the molar mass.

examples.explanation: Mass spectrum shows molecular ion at $m/z = 180$, confirming molar mass $180.16~\text{g/mol}$ for $\text{C}_6\text{H}_{12}\text{O}_6$.

image.caption: Molecular ion peak gives molar mass.

examples.explanation: Molecular ion at $m/z = 60$ suggests molar mass $\approx 60~\text{g/mol}$; combined with empirical $\text{CH}_2\text{O}$ gives molecular $\text{C}_2\text{H}_4\text{O}_2$.

image.caption: Mass spec enables molecular formula determination.

categories.description: Measures the lowering of a solvent's freezing point when solute is added; the magnitude depends on solute molar mass.
categories.detailed_mechanism: $\Delta T_f = K_f \times m \times i$, where $m$ is molality. For non-electrolytes ($i = 1$), molar mass = $\dfrac{\text{mass solute} \times K_f}{\Delta T_f \times \text{mass solvent (kg)}}$.

examples.explanation: $1.50~\text{g}$ solute in $25.0~\text{g}$ benzene lowers fp by $2.56^\circ\text{C}$; $K_f = 5.12^\circ\text{C}/m$. Molar mass = $\dfrac{1.50 \times 5.12}{2.56 \times 0.0250} = 120~\text{g/mol}$.

categories.description: Measures the density of a compound's vapor at known T and P; uses ideal gas law to calculate molar mass.
categories.detailed_mechanism: From $PV = nRT$ and $n = m/M$: $M = \dfrac{mRT}{PV} = \dfrac{dRT}{P}$, where $d$ is vapor density.

image.caption: Gas law determines molar mass from vapor density.

principles.principle: Method Selection Depends on Compound Properties
principles.statement: Choose molar mass determination method based on compound volatility, stability, solubility, and available sample amount.
principles.application: Volatile compounds: vapor density; non-volatile organics: mass spec or freezing point; aqueous solutions: osmotic pressure.
principles.example: Understanding method limitations ensures accurate molar mass data for molecular formula determination.

principles.statement: Molar mass measurements have uncertainty; $n$ should round to a whole number within experimental error. Large deviations indicate problems.
principles.application: If $n = 2.98 \pm 0.05$, round to 3. If $n = 2.5 \pm 0.1$, recheck data—error is too large for confident assignment.
principles.analogy: Like measuring a table: if you get $120.2~\text{cm}$ with $\pm 0.5~\text{cm}$ error, report $120~\text{cm}$. If you get $122.5~\text{cm}$, remeasure.

sections.section_title: Worked Examples: Complete Molecular Formula Determination
sections.introduction: Apply the complete analytical workflow: percent composition to empirical formula, plus molar mass to molecular formula. These examples demonstrate real-world compound identification.

worked_examples.title: Complete Analysis: From Percent Composition to Molecular Formula
worked_examples.problem: Combustion of $0.250~\text{g}$ of a compound containing C, H, and O produces $0.567~\text{g}~\text{CO}_2$ and $0.232~\text{g}~\text{H}_2\text{O}$. Mass spectrometry gives molar mass $88.11~\text{g/mol}$. Determine the molecular formula.

step_by_step.step: Calculate Mass of O by Difference
Convert to Moles and Find Empirical Formula

step_by_step.explanation: Empirical mass: $3 \times 12.01 + 6 \times 1.008 + 16.00 = 58.08~\text{g/mol}$. $n = \dfrac{88.11}{58.08} = 1.52 \approx 1.5$. Multiply by 2: $n = 3$ for whole numbers.
step_by_step.calculation: Wait—recheck: $88.11/58.08 = 1.517$. Not whole. Recheck empirical formula.

step_by_step.explanation: Mole ratio C:H:O = 0.0129:0.0258:0.0043 = 3:6:1. Empirical $\text{C}_3\text{H}_6\text{O}$ is correct. Molar mass $88.11 \div 58.08 = 1.52$. Close to 1.5, so multiply empirical by 2: $\text{C}_6\text{H}_{12}\text{O}_2$, mass = $116.16$—too high. Try $n = 1.5 \times 2 = 3$: $\text{C}_9\text{H}_{18}\text{O}_3$, mass = $174.24$—wrong. Recalculate: $88.11/58.08 = 1.517 \approx 1.5$. Multiply empirical by 1.5: not allowed. Conclusion: experimental error or wrong empirical.

Actual calculation: $88.11/58.08 = 1.517$. Within 1% of 1.5, but molecular formulas require whole $n$. Recheck combustion data or molar mass. If molar mass is actually $116.16~\text{g/mol}$, then $n = 2$ and molecular = $\text{C}_6\text{H}_{12}\text{O}_2$.

worked_examples.final_answer: Assuming the molar mass is $116.16~\text{g/mol}$ (not $88.11$), the molecular formula is $\text{C}_6\text{H}_{12}\text{O}_2$. This example illustrates the importance of consistent data: if $n$ is not whole, recheck all measurements. With correct data, the workflow is: combustion analysis → empirical formula → molar mass → molecular formula.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Complete Analytical Workflow with Error Checking

worked_examples.title: Molecular Formula from Empirical and Molar Mass
worked_examples.problem: A compound has empirical formula $\text{C}_2\text{H}_4\text{N}$ and molar mass $84.12~\text{g/mol}$. Determine the molecular formula and identify the compound if possible.

step_by_step.explanation: $\text{C}_4\text{H}_8\text{N}_2$ could be succinonitrile ($\text{NCCH}_2\text{CH}_2\text{CN}$), 1,2-diaminocyclobutane, or other isomers. Additional structural data needed for unique identification.

worked_examples.final_answer: The molecular formula is $\text{C}_4\text{H}_8\text{N}_2$. The exact $n = 2$ confirms data consistency. This formula represents several possible structural isomers; spectroscopic methods (IR, NMR) would distinguish them.

worked_examples.title: Hydrate Molecular Formula Determination
worked_examples.problem: A hydrate has empirical formula $\text{CuSO}_4 \cdot 5\text{H}_2\text{O}$ and molar mass $249.72~\text{g/mol}$. Verify the molecular formula.

step_by_step.explanation: For hydrates, the empirical formula often equals the molecular formula because the water molecules are part of the crystal structure, not separate molecules.

worked_examples.final_answer: The molecular formula is $\text{CuSO}_4 \cdot 5\text{H}_2\text{O}$ (copper(II) sulfate pentahydrate). For hydrates, the empirical formula typically represents the actual formula unit in the crystal, so $n = 1$ is common.

worked_examples.title: Comparing Compounds with Same Empirical Formula
worked_examples.problem: Three compounds all have empirical formula $\text{CH}_2\text{O}$. Their molar masses are: A: $30.03~\text{g/mol}$, B: $60.05~\text{g/mol}$, C: $180.16~\text{g/mol}$. Determine molecular formulas and suggest identities.

step_by_step.explanation: A: Formaldehyde ($\text{HCHO}$); B: Acetic acid ($\text{CH}_3\text{COOH}$) or methyl formate; C: Glucose, fructose, or galactose.

worked_examples.final_answer: Molecular formulas: A: $\text{CH}_2\text{O}$ (formaldehyde), B: $\text{C}_2\text{H}_4\text{O}_2$ (acetic acid), C: $\text{C}_6\text{H}_{12}\text{O}_6$ (glucose). This example shows how the same empirical formula can represent very different compounds with different properties, distinguished only by molar mass.

worked_examples.title: Verification: Molecular Mass from Molecular Formula
worked_examples.problem: Verify that the molecular formula $\text{C}_8\text{H}_{10}\text{N}_4\text{O}_2$ has molar mass $194.19~\text{g/mol}$ (caffeine).
worked_examples.final_answer: The calculated molar mass of $\text{C}_8\text{H}_{10}\text{N}_4\text{O}_2$ is $194.20~\text{g/mol}$, matching the given $194.19~\text{g/mol}$ within rounding error. This verification step confirms the molecular formula is consistent with the experimental molar mass.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Molar Mass Verification for Molecular Formula

sections.introduction: Avoid these frequent errors when determining molecular formulas; apply expert strategies for accurate analytical work.

key_concept.definition: Common molecular formula errors include: not checking that $n$ is whole-numbered, multiplying only some subscripts by $n$, using wrong atomic masses, and forgetting to verify the final molar mass.
key_concept.context: Careful calculation and verification prevent errors that lead to incorrect compound identification.

classifications.type: Mistake: Not Checking That $n$ Is Whole
characteristics: Not rounding to nearest whole number
classifications.behavior: Molecular formulas must have whole-number subscripts. Non-whole $n$ indicates calculation error, wrong empirical formula, or inaccurate molar mass.

examples.explanation: Wrong: use $n = 2.48$. Correct: round to $n = 2$ (if within error) or recheck data. Molecular formulas can't have 2.48 atoms.

image.caption: $n$ must be whole-numbered for valid molecular formula.

characteristics: Multiplying only C and H, forgetting O
classifications.behavior: The multiplier $n$ applies to ALL subscripts in the empirical formula. Partial multiplication distorts the atom ratio.

image.caption: Multiply every subscript by $n$, not just some.

characteristics: Using atomic number instead of atomic mass
Getting wrong empirical or molecular mass
classifications.behavior: Atomic mass precision affects empirical formula mass and $n$ calculation. Use standard values (4 sig figs) throughout.

examples.explanation: Wrong: use 6 (atomic number) or 12 (rounded). Correct: use 12.01 (standard atomic mass). Small errors compound in $n$ calculation.

image.caption: Use atomic mass (bottom number) for calculations.

characteristics: Not comparing to given molar mass
classifications.behavior: Verification catches multiplication errors, wrong $n$, or empirical formula mistakes before finalizing the answer.

image.caption: Always verify molecular mass matches given value.

classifications.type: Pro Tip: Use the Molecular Formula Checklist
characteristics: $n$ = molar mass / empirical mass = whole number? Check.
classifications.behavior: This systematic check catches errors before submitting answers or proceeding with compound identification.

examples.explanation: Empirical $\text{C}_2\text{H}_4\text{N}$ ✓; Empirical mass 42.06 ✓; $n = 84.12/42.06 = 2$ ✓; All subscripts × 2 ✓; Molecular mass 84.12 ✓.

image.caption: Checklist ensures thorough molecular formula determination.

characteristics: Use 4 sig figs for atomic masses
Keep extra digits in intermediate calculations
Round only the final molecular formula subscripts
classifications.behavior: Early rounding can convert $n = 1.99$ to $2.0$ (ok) or $n = 2.49$ to $2.5$ (problem). Maintain precision to correctly identify whole-number $n$.

examples.explanation: Use: empirical mass = 30.03 (not 30); molar mass = 180.16 (not 180); $n = 180.16/30.03 = 6.00$ (exact). Early rounding could give $n = 180/30 = 6$ (ok here) but fail for borderline cases.

image.caption: Keep precision to correctly identify whole-number $n$.

principles.statement: The multiplier $n = \dfrac{\text{Molar Mass}}{\text{Empirical Formula Mass}}$ must equal (or round to) a whole number within experimental uncertainty. Non-whole results require data rechecking.
principles.application: If $n = 2.98 \pm 0.03$, round to 3. If $n = 2.5 \pm 0.1$, the uncertainty is too large—recheck empirical formula or molar mass measurement.
principles.analogy: Like counting eggs: you can have 2 or 3 eggs, but not 2.5 eggs. Molecular formulas require whole atoms.

principles.principle: Molecular Formula Uniqueness Requires Both Data Types
principles.statement: Empirical formula alone cannot uniquely identify a compound; molar mass is required to determine the multiplier $n$ and thus the molecular formula.
principles.application: $\text{CH}_2\text{O}$ could be formaldehyde ($n = 1$), acetic acid ($n = 2$), or glucose ($n = 6$). Molar mass distinguishes them.
principles.example: This is why analytical chemistry combines multiple techniques: combustion analysis for empirical formula, mass spectrometry for molar mass.

summary: The molecular formula shows the actual number of atoms in a molecule and is always a whole-number multiple of the empirical formula: $\text{Molecular Formula} = n \times \text{Empirical Formula}$, where $n = \dfrac{\text{Molar Mass}}{\text{Empirical Formula Mass}}$. Calculation steps: (1) calculate empirical formula mass from atomic masses, (2) divide experimental molar mass by empirical mass to get $n$, (3) multiply all empirical subscripts by $n$ to get molecular formula, (4) verify by recalculating molecular mass. The multiplier $n$ must be a whole number (1, 2, 3, ...); non-whole results indicate calculation errors or inaccurate data. Molar mass is determined experimentally by mass spectrometry (most precise), freezing point depression, vapor density, or other colligative property methods. Multiple compounds can share the same empirical formula but have different molecular formulas and properties (e.g., formaldehyde $\text{CH}_2\text{O}$, acetic acid $\text{C}_2\text{H}_4\text{O}_2$, glucose $\text{C}_6\text{H}_{12}\text{O}_6$); molar mass distinguishes them. Critical skills include: maintaining calculation precision, recognizing when $n$ rounds to a whole number, multiplying all subscripts by $n$, and verifying the final molecular mass matches the given value. Common mistakes include accepting non-whole $n$ values, multiplying only some subscripts, using wrong atomic masses, and skipping verification. Mastery of molecular formula determination is essential for identifying unknown compounds and connecting analytical data to chemical structure.
